title: Geopolitics
description: Combined think-tank feed: ISW, RUSI, CSIS, and the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.

items.title: What We Know About the U.S.-Saudi Nuclear Deal
items.content_text: Despite the agreement’s uncertainty, it’s still a significant development in regional and nuclear policy.

items.title: Burnham Has a Narrow Window to Shape UK AI Policy
items.content_text: His challenge will come in balancing domestic priorities with a sharpening geopolitical environment.

items.title: A Path Forward on AI Safety for the United States and China
items.content_text: “AI safety in parallel” is modest and pragmatic but vital.

items.title: Negotiating an Age of Conquest: Converting American Power Into Durable Political Outcomes
items.content_text: There is a deeply rooted assumption in post–Cold War American statecraft that overwhelming military and economic power translates directly into favorable political outcomes. But force alone is rarely enough. To meet the challenges of the mid-21st century, Washington needs a cultural shift toward a mindset that takes negotiation far more seriously.

items.title: The Alternative für Deutschland and Germany’s Unsettled Place in the World
items.content_text: Unresolved tensions within the AfD may ultimately constrain the its potential as a leader among European and global populist forces.

items.title: A Competitive Analysis of the Biomanufacturing Strategic Landscape and Technology Stack
items.content_text: Biomanufacturing—the ability to deploy biotechnology for manufacturing—is rapidly emerging as a global economic and national security priority.

items.title: Why Europe Must Take Charge of its Russia Diplomacy Now
items.content_text: Trump is distracted and Ukraine wants Europe to step up. The continent’s leaders must find their voice and assert it in talks with Russia.

items.title: India’s Stake in the Global Subsea Cable Race
items.content_text: The Indian Ocean Region hosts one of the densest concentrations of subsea cables in the world. Yet, despite accounting for a significant share of global internet traffic, India’s participation in the ownership, routing, and maintenance of this network remains limited.

items.title: Detour Dividends: How Syrian Airspace Benefits from the Iran War
items.content_text: This installment of Charting the Middle East spotlights the unlikely positive implications of flight disruptions for Syria in wake of the Iran war.

items.title: How the U.S. Can Survive a Large-Scale AI Cyberattack
items.content_text: Advances in offensive AI cyber capabilities are threatening U.S. cybersecurity. The U.S. must improve its whole-of-society cyber readiness through enhancing its partnerships and communication before a major AI cyber-attack occurs.

items.title: Will the U.S.-Saudi Nuclear Deal Proceed?
items.content_text: President Trump's last-minute link between the U.S.-Saudi nuclear agreement and Israel normalization injects new uncertainty into an already contentious deal.

items.title: Losing Control of the West’s Longstanding Interests in Seychelles
items.content_text: A democratic and well-functioning international financial centre that has benefited from Western support, Seychelles seems to now be choosing its friends elsewhere.

items.title: North Africa’s Flaring Problem is Europe’s Gas Opportunity
items.content_text: The fallout from the Iran War has not been kind to European economies. A UK leadership change presents an opportunity to invest in combatting gas flaring in North Africa, so as to bolster security of supply, and tie ongoing international diplomatic efforts on Libya to that agenda.

items.title: Where Are President Tokayev’s Political Reforms Leading Kazakhstan?
items.content_text: Tokayev’s predecessor, Nursultan Nazarbayev, outlined arrangements for a power transition too soon and in too much detail, ultimately losing control over the process. Tokayev is determined not to meet the same fate.

items.title: States Weekly Newsletter: July 22, 2026
items.content_text: This edition of States Weekly covers July 22, 2026.

items.title: Charting a New Course: Countering China's Dominance in Global Shipbuilding
items.content_text: China has developed the largest shipbuilding industry in recent history and now uses it as an instrument of national power. It is key to Beijing's ambitions to displace the United States as the preeminent power in the Asia-Pacific and project power far from China’s shores.

items.title: Section 232 Explained
items.content_text: In this week's column, Bill Reinsch explains how Section 232 national security investigations have become popular trade tool of the Trump administration, arguing that while some tariffs are difficult to justify, recent decisions suggest a more selective approach to their use.

items.title: Tracking Global Compulsory Licensing Trends in Biopharmaceuticals
items.content_text: From HIV/AIDS to Covid-19, global health emergencies have revived calls for compulsory licensing. But history shows that innovation, technology transfer, and manufacturing capacity—not patent waivers alone—drive access to lifesaving medicines.

items.title: What Do the Election Security Documents Say About Chinese Interference in U.S. Elections?
items.content_text: The biggest takeaway from the declassified intelligence isn't a cover-up—it's how intelligence analysis actually works. Healthy disagreement, documented dissent, and rigorous debate are signs of a strong intelligence community, not a broken one.

items.title: AI Strategies and the Informal Economy: Africa’s Job Creation Test
items.content_text: African nations have implemented AI strategies that focus on their formal sectors, leaving the informal economy behind.

items.title: Russia’s Selective Adaptation: Command, Control and the Limits of Learning in War
items.content_text: The contrast between Russia’s tactical flexibility and operational rigidity is revealing of the insecurity in its executive function.

items.title: Japan's Ambivalent Policy Towards Russia and Ukraine
items.content_text: Prime Minister Takaichi’s equivocal stance on policy towards Russia has encouraged a revival in activity from those in Japan who seek reengagement with Moscow.

items.title: Is AI as Bad for the Environment as Everyone Thinks?
items.content_text: That statistic about a bottle of water may not live up to scrutiny.

items.title: Building Mini-Refineries Will Not Solve Russia’s Gasoline Crisis
items.content_text: Official discussions about mini oil refineries are unlikely to come to anything—but the mere fact they’re happening reveals the regime is failing to deliver a functioning economy.

items.title: Why Russia Could Escalate Hybrid Warfare Against Europe
items.content_text: Mounting economic strain and battlefield losses will not necessarily make the Kremlin less dangerous. They could instead push Moscow toward a more aggressive hybrid campaign designed to test NATO’s Eastern flank, exploit allied hesitation, and fracture European resolve.

items.content_text: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign is putting pressure on Russia's energy infrastructure and forcing increasing political pain for Putin.

items.title: Taking the Pulse: Enough with the Annual NATO Summits, Already?
items.content_text: Over the past ten years, NATO has held almost as many summits as it did during the entirety of the Cold War. Are they still useful, or is it time to stop holding annual meetings?

items.title: The UK's Defence Investment Plan Has a Lethal Drone Swarm Vision
items.content_text: Drone swarming is often referenced as an important future capability, with little understanding of what is possible today, and how the tactic is enabled.

items.content_text: The move is an escalation of its war on multilateralism.

items.title: Europe from Scratch: Visions for a New European Order
items.content_text: Several leaders have called for major EU reforms, but only modest changes have resulted. What if the European order really could be reimagined from zero today?

items.title: What Should We Take From Andrey Melnichenko’s Essay in the Economist?
items.content_text: Andrey Melnichenko’s essay offers no answer to the fundamental question of how, under any kind of negotiated settlement, Europe can protect itself from the Russian ressentiment that is inevitable in all scenarios except for an outright victory for Putin.

items.title: Cheap Attack, Expensive Defence: NATO 5% Pledge Following Iran and Ukraine
items.content_text: Iran and Ukraine show cheap mass defeats expensive platforms. Unless it buys deep, low-cost defence and civilian resilience, NATO can win every engagement and still lose wars.

items.content_text: Moscow’s strategic communications cast Russia as a backseat passenger on a road to war in Europe – but war is not inevitable.

items.title: Episode 124: After Ankara: First Reflections on the 2026 NATO Summit
items.content_text: The 2026 NATO Summit convened at a moment of fragile unity for the alliance. What did it deliver? What did it leave unresolved?

items.title: The AI-Enhanced Land Carrier Battle Group: The Way Forward
items.content_text: The historical evolution of naval aviation offers useful ideas for overcoming the attritional nature of current drone and AI-dominated land warfare

items.title: 25 Years After 9/11. The Next Global Shock Could be Infinitely Worse
items.content_text: The next global shock may be the use of nuclear weapons assisted by the more casual attitudes of less responsible world leaders and the lack of popular understanding and fear.

items.title: The Future of Russian Sanctions After the Hormuz Crisis
items.content_text: US–Iran peace negotiations have stumbled, but if normalcy returns to the Strait of Hormuz, the G7 must be ready to align again on pressuring Russia.

items.title: Threats to Maritime Trade are Spreading to the Indo-Pacific
items.content_text: The ‘Bashi Breakout’ – China’s maritime operation east of Taiwan – is a warning that disruption to maritime trade is spreading to the Indo-Pacific.

items.content_text: China’s ‘war by other means’ is there to see, if Europe looks past isolated incidents.

items.content_text: Ukraine’s use of drone warfare has serious implications for Russian lines of communication.

items.title: NATO 3.0 to Emerge at Ankara Summit Amid Fragile Unity
items.content_text: European allies and Canada will try to ease recurring tensions with President Trump by stepping up in a rebalanced alliance: NATO 3.0. But transatlantic unity remains fragile.

items.title: Ramzan Kadyrov: The Kremlin’s Messenger to the Islamic World
items.content_text: The reported failing health of Ramzan Kadyrov leaves a strategic communications gap for a Kremlin speaking to the Islamic world.
